coredata.dc:title: Different types of Nitrogen-doped CQDs loaded by CoP used as OER electrocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Shuang
Wang, Xinyu
Zhu, Yaqing
He, Pinyi
Yu, Xu
Qin, Fu
Yao, Yongkang
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, CoP catalysts loaded with N-doped carbon dots (NCQDs) have gained attention as promising metal-free electrocatalysts due to their low cost, simple preparation process, and excellent electrocatalytic performance. However, few studies have investigated the effect of different types of nitrogen doping on the catalytic performance of NCQDs in electrocatalysis. In this study, NCQDs doped with graphite/amine N, pyridine N, and pyrrole/amine/pyridine N were prepared using various small molecule precursor systems, and control experiments were conducted with CQDs without nitrogen doping. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that nitrogen doping can enhance the electrocatalytic performance of the NCQDs. Notably, CoP modified with pyrrole/amine/pyridine N-doped CQDs (NCQDs-3) exhibited significantly improved electrocatalytic performance, the largest specific surface area, and the lowest charge transfer resistance. Pyridine N was found to increase structural defects, pyrrole N could supply electrons to create an electron-rich state in CoP, while amine and graphite N could reduce the bandgap. Furthermore, the Co-N-C covalent bonds between CoP and CQDs enhanced conductivity. Therefore, the electrochemical performance of the electrocatalyst was closely related to the synergy between the nitrogen atom doping mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen atom
Doping type
Carbon quantum dots
Cobalt phosphide
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis process of CoP-CQDs and CoP-NCQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) CoP-CQDs, (b) CoP-NCQDs-1, (c) CoP-NCQDs-2 and (d) CoP-NCQDs-3.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTREM image, (c)HAADF, (d) C, (e) N, (f) O, (g) Co and (h) P element mapping images of CoP-NCQDs-3.
(a) FT-IR spectra, the high-resolution XPS pattern of (b) Survey, (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s of CQDs, NCQDs-1, NCQDs-2 and NCQDs-3.
The high-resolution XPS pattern of (a) Survey, (b) Co 2p, (c) P 2p and (d) N 1 s of CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1, CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3.

The OER (a) overpotential histogram of pure CoP, CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1, CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3 catalysts; (b) Cdl values, (c) Roughness factor (left) and ECSA (right), and (d) Nyquist plots of CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1, CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3 catalysts.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of all CQDs and CoP-NCQDs.

head.ce:title: Different types of Nitrogen-doped CQDs loaded by CoP used as OER electrocatalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, CoP catalysts loaded with N-doped carbon dots (NCQDs) have gained attention as promising metal-free electrocatalysts due to their low cost, simple preparation process, and excellent electrocatalytic performance. However, few studies have investigated the effect of different types of nitrogen doping on the catalytic performance of NCQDs in electrocatalysis. In this study, NCQDs doped with graphite/amine N, pyridine N, and pyrrole/amine/pyridine N were prepared using various small molecule precursor systems, and control experiments were conducted with CQDs without nitrogen doping. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that nitrogen doping can enhance the electrocatalytic performance of the NCQDs. Notably, CoP modified with pyrrole/amine/pyridine N-doped CQDs (NCQDs-3) exhibited significantly improved electrocatalytic performance, the largest specific surface area, and the lowest charge transfer resistance. Pyridine N was found to increase structural defects, pyrrole N could supply electrons to create an electron-rich state in CoP, while amine and graphite N could reduce the bandgap. Furthermore, the Co-N-C covalent bonds between CoP and CQDs enhanced conductivity. Therefore, the electrochemical performance of the electrocatalyst was closely related to the synergy between the nitrogen atom doping mode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the use of conventional fuels has caused a rise in serious environmental issues. Therefore, it is imperative to accelerate the development of renewable and clean energy sources [1,2]. It is now widely believed that the most promising technology for hydrogen and oxygen production is the electrocatalytic water splitting technology, which consists of the anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [3–6]. The OER suffers from issues such as high over-potential and poor reaction kinetics. Currently, noble metal-based catalysts such as RuO2 and IrO2 are frequently used to enhance the catalytic activity of OER. However, their high cost and poor stability have hindered their large-scale promotion [7,8].

Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) are gaining attention in recent years due to their low cost, simple preparation, high electrocatalytic activity, suitable d-orbital electronic structure, bond energy, and rich chemical state [9,10]. Among TMPs, CoP is an important member that can promote the adsorption and desorption of hydrogen or oxygen in the reaction and has excellent electrocatalytic performance. However, compared with noble metal-based catalysts, TMPs catalysts have poor conductivity, tend to agglomerate, and exhibit poor corrosion resistance, which affects their electrocatalytic activity [11].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a small size of only a few nanometers. In contrast to other carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, CQDs have excellent characteristics due to their carbon core and surface groups. The graphitized core provides good conductivity, while the surface rich in functional groups can coordinate and cross-link with metal ions to solve the problems of poor conductivity and easy aggregation of CoP materials [12]. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, boron, fluorine, and chlorine can increase electron flow. Nitrogen atoms, in particular, are easy to obtain and low cost and are often used as dopants [13–16]. Recent research has shown that the charge distribution and electron transfer of carbon atoms can be controlled by doping nitrogen atoms, which significantly improves the electrochemical performance of the composite catalyst [17–19]. Nitrogen atoms also contain an electron lone pair that can capture metal ions for coordination bonding [20–22]. Researchers have successfully coupled nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NGQDs) with Ni(Fe)OxHy nanosheets to enhance the kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [23]. Additionally, boron quantum dots (BNQDs) have been densely assembled on the surface of CoP nanosheets/carbon cloth (CC) to improve the electrocatalytic performance of nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) [24]. Nitrogen-doped carbon nanofibers have been used to create bifunctional catalysts for oxygen electrochemistry by doping ultra-small CoP nanoparticles [25]. The various forms of nitrogen doping, including graphite N, pyrrole N, amine N, and pyridine N, have been studied to determine the type of nitrogen atom doping that has the greatest impact on CQDs and is most conducive to enhancing the electrocatalytic activity [26–27]. Further research is required to understand the function of nitrogen atom doping in the electrocatalytic procedure and design and optimize efficient nitrogen-doped non-metallic electrocatalysts.

Here, CQDs doped with graphite/amine N, pyridine N and pyrrole/amine/pyridine N were prepared with ammonia, o-phenylenediamine, DTPA and glycine as dopants, respectively. In addition, to illustrate the importance of nitrogen atom, no dopant is used to prepare nitrogen free CQDs. Simple hydrothermal treatment and phosphating were used to prepare CoP-CQDs /CoP-NCQDs composite electrocatalysts. Electrochemical experiments showed that the N content in CQDs significantly affected the CoP-NCQDs electrocatalytic performance. The doping modes of nitrogen atoms (intranuclear doping, surface state) synergistically promotes the electrochemical performance of CoP-NCQDs composites, and pyrrole N doping is most conducive to optimizing the electrochemical performance of OER reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis process of CoP-CQDs and CoP-NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process of CoP-NCQDs catalysts is shown in Scheme 1 . Using the bottom-up method, various CQDs were prepared using citric acid, citric acid and ammonia, o-phenylenediamine, DTPA and glycine as small molecular precursors. Among them, ammonia, o-phenylenediamine and DPTA are used as nitrogen sources respectively to obtain different types of nitrogen-doped NCQDs. In addition, CQDs obtained from precursor systems without nitrogen sources are used as blank controls to illustrate the importance of nitrogen doping. CoP-NCQDs are obtained by the classical two-step method of hydrothermal and phosphating. Firstly, the precursor of NCQDs modified cobalt carbonate hydroxide (Co(OH)2-NCQDs) was successfully synthesized using cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, urea and ammonium fluoride. Among them, hydroxides and carbonates produced by thermal decomposition of urea can combine with Co2+ to form Co(OH)2 [11,28]. Subsequently, CoP-NCQDs arrays were prepared by phosphating the Co(OH)2-NCQDs precursors with NaH2PO2.

The luminescence characteristics of all CQDs before and after hybridization with CoP were studied. From Fig. S1 a-d (Supporting Information), the four kinds of CQDs show strong blue/green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (λ = 365 nm, inset). The UV–Visible absorption spectra of the four different CQDs aqueous dispersions and the hybridized catalysts show that the π - π* transition of aromatic C = C in CQDs and NCQDs appeared at around 238 nm, while the peaks near 290 nm and 330 nm match n-π* transition of C = O and C = N parts, respectively [29,30]. It is worth noting that after the combination of CQDs and CoP, the fluorescence is quenched, and the excitation peak disappears, which may be owing to the electrostatic interaction between the positively and negatively charged CQDs of CoP [24]. This shows the successful synthesis of a series of CoP-CQDs composites.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of composite catalysts (Fig. 1a-d) show the morphology of nanoflakes stacked into nanoflowers, which indicates that N-doping hardly affects the basic structure of the catalysts. It is worth noting that compared with the CoP-CQDs without nitrogen doping, the nanolayers of CoP-NCQDs-1/2/3 catalysts are more and more densely stacked, increasing the surface area by forming the 3D structure of nanoflowers, thus increasing the active sites. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) further observed the nanostructure morphology about the composites. The TEM images (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S2a-c) and SEM images can be well corresponded, showing the nanoflower structure. A few minor black dots are scattered of the nano petals. Through HRTEM images, the black dots show obvious lattice stripes with crystal plane spacing of 0.19 and 0.21 nm, equivalent to the (103) and (100) crystal planes of graphitized carbon (JCPDS. 26–1080) [31]. Generally, graphitized core of NCQDs is beneficial to improve conductivity and accelerate charge transfer. In addition, the HRTEM images shows 0.18 and 0.26 nm lattice spacing, corresponding to the CoP (211) and (400) crystal plane, which confirms the successful combination of CQDs and CoP [32]. According to the element distribution mapping obtained by high-angle ring dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM; Fig. 2c-h), the elements C, N, O, Co and P can be found to be evenly distributed in the nanoflower-like nanostructures. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3 a) was tested to understand the surface functional groups of four CQDs and CoP-CQDs. The broad peaks at around 3400 and 2987 cm−1 are ascribed to O–H and C–H peaks. The absorption bands at 1776 and 1718 cm−1, 1629 and 1457 cm−1 are accredited to C = O and aromatic C = C stretching. The absorption bands at 1200 and 1120 cm−1 are accredited to aromatic heterocyclic aliphatic C-O-C and C-O stretching vibrations, the C = N, CO-NH and C-N peaks are observed at about 1675, 1571 and 1405 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the surface of the NCQDs-1 and NCQDs-3 samples contains major –NH2 (≈3180 and ≈673 cm−1) and amide bond. It can be seen that only CQDs do not have nitrogen functional groups. In addition, the characteristic peaks of CoP-CQDs (Fig. S3) almost all exist in the FTIR spectrum of CQDs. Therefore, FTIR results show that CQDs with abundant surface functional groups have been successfully doped into CoP. It has been reported that CQDs will be compounded on the surface of CoP through van der Waals force [15]. The phase and composition of CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1, CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3 were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. S4 displays that the diffraction peaks at around 31.6° (011), 36.3° (111), 46.2° (112), 48.1° (211) and 56.0° (020) are well matched with the characteristic diffraction peaks of CoP crystal (PDF#29–0497), which proves that the composites have been successfully synthesized. In addition, no obvious diffraction peak related to CQDs is observed due to the low crystal phase diffraction intensity and small size of CQDs [24,33].

It should be noted that the function of CQDs depends on the core and surface functionalization, and the nitrogen atom is affected by the nitrogen doping level and the relative place of the nitrogen atom [34–36]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can understand the surface composition and chemical state of CQDs and CoP-CQDs, even the type of nitrogen doping. The XPS survey spectrum for CQDs confirms the presence of C, N, and O at binding energies around 284 eV, 399 eV and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). Deconvolution of the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals the C–C/C–H (284.1 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), C-O (285.5 eV) and C = O (287.4 eV) [37–40]. Compared with CQDs, the C-O bond in NCQDs-1/3 can have a slight negative shift, which may be related to the existence of amine functional groups and the formation of hydrogen bonds or amide bonds (CO-NH) with hydroxyl groups (C-OH groups) [41,42]. In addition, the binding energy of C = O also has a certain shift, which may be due to the interaction between CQDs containing (O = C-OH) carboxyl functional group and pyrrole N, where pyrrole N donates electrons, forming an electron flow moving from pyrrole N to C = O bond, and accelerating electron conduction. Therefore, NCQDs-3 contains both amine N and pyrrole N, which may lead to a greater degree of electron transfer. Notably, the NCQDs-2 sample uses o-phenylenediamine as the precursor without oxygen containing functional group. This is consistent with FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3a) results. N1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows that NCQDs-1 has two peaks, namely amine N (38.8%) and graphite N (61.2%). NCQDs-2 has three peaks, namely pyridine N (67.2%), amine N (19.5%) and pyrrole N (13.3%). NCQDs-3 has three peaks, namely pyridine N (48.3%), amine N (36.7%), and pyrrole N (15.0%) [43–45]. In addition, no relevant peak was found in the N 1 s spectra of CQDs prepared with citric acid only, which indicates that there is no nitrogen atom in CQDs. Previous studies have shown that in different types of nitrogen doping, graphite N, pyrrole N and amine N can improve electron conduction. Pyridine N is mainly doped on the surface, thus increasing structural defects and introducing more active sites [46,47,65]. In addition, NCQDs-2 contains only pyridine N, which may have more structural defects.

The XPS Survey, N 1 s, O 1 s, Co 2p and P 2p spectra for CoP-CQDs and CoP-NCQDs were depicted in Fig. 4 a-d. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4a) reveals the Co 2p (798 eV), O 1 s (5318 eV), P 2p (133 eV) and N 1 s (400 eV), indicating the elemental composition of the four catalysts. In the Co 2p spectra (Fig. 4b), Co3+ 2p3/2 and Co3+ 2p1/2 (Co-P) correspond to peaks at 777.73 and 792.23 eV [48]. The O 1 s spectra found that all catalysts contain two peaks, corresponding to C = O and C-O (Fig.S5). Co2+ 2p3/2 and Co2+ 2p1/2 (Co-N) correspond to the peaks at 781.38 and 796.98 eV. In addition, CoP-NCQDs composites may be oxidized by air due to contact with air, so satellite peaks appear at 785.43 and 801.18 eV [49]. The P 2p spectra (Fig. 4c) contains three peaks with binding energies of 129.48 eV and 128.33 eV corresponding to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 [50]. 137.7 eV satellite peak is caused by the oxidation of CoP-NCQDs in the air to produce P-O bonds [51]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 4d) contains four peaks, corresponding to pyridine N, amine N/Co-N-C, pyrrole N and graphite N [52]. In addition, in order to more directly illustrate the influence of different nitrogen atom types on catalytic performance, different nitrogen contents and FWHM are further summarized in Table S1 and Table S2. It was concluded that CoP-CQDs and CoP-NCQDs-1 only contained graphite N and amine N, while CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3 also contained pyrrole N and pyridine N. CoP-NCQDs-2 had the highest pyridine N content, and CoP-NCQDs-3 had the highest pyrrole N content. It is noteworthy that compared with CoP-CQDs catalyst, the peaks of CoP-NCQDs composite in the Co 2p and P 2p spectra have a certain negative shift, indicating that the addition of nitrogen atoms can alter the charge distribution of CQDs and accelerate electron conduction [18,23,28,53]. In addition, additional lone-pair electrons can coordinate with Co atoms to form an electron transfer channel for Co-N-C [54,55]. The binding energy of Co3+metal peak and P 2p3/2 exhibits 0.55/0.85 eV, 0.40/0.30 eV and 0.45/0.20 eV negative shift (Co-P) for the three CoP-NCQDs samples, respectively, indicating that Co atoms in CoP-NCQDs are in electron-rich state compared to that in CoP-CQDs, thus optimizing the electronic structure of the CoP center. On the contrary, the obvious shift to high binding energy of amine N/Co-N-C peaks, indicating that CoP and CQDs form strong electronic interactions through Co-N-C covalent bonds and nitrogen-doped CQDs can effectively accelerate electronic conduction. Among them, the CoP-NCQDs-3 sample exhibits the largest displacement and is the most effective. According to XPS fitting results, different atomic contents are summarized in Table S3. The shift trend of amine N/Co-N-C bond binding energy corresponds to the amount of N atom content. These changes in the binding energies of Co and N indicate partial electrons transfer from N atoms to Co atoms at the CoP/NCQDs interfaces through Co-N-C covalent bonds, and nitrogen-doped CQDs may effectively accelerate electronic conduction.

As shown by Raman spectra (Fig. S6), the peaks at ∼ 1321 and ∼ 1568 cm−1 correspond to defect (D) and graphite (G) bands, respectively. In the carbon structure, the G-band is due to the vibration of sp2 carbon atom, and the D-band is due to the related vibration of the edge defect of the skeleton [56,57]. Obviously, the ID/IG ratio of CoP-CQDs without nitrogen-doped CQDs is only 0.9, but the ratio of CoP-NCQDs-2 is as high as 4.63, indicating that the CoP-CQDs sample has a higher graphitization structure, while CoP-NCQDs-2 has more defect structures. This is consistent with the results of XPS N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d) analysis. The N2 adsorption–desorption curves was tested for understand the specific surface area and pore structure about the composites. Fig. S7a shows that the isotherms of the four samples are similar. Under low pressure, they belong to the single-layer filling of micropores, and the medium pressure part belongs to the initial small amount of multi-layer adsorption on the outer surface of mesopores or macropores [58], all of which have hysteresis loops. To reveal additional structural characteristics, pore size distribution (PSD) curves were analyzed. It can be observed that the PSD (Fig. S7b) of CoP-NCQDs-2 and CoP-NCQDs-3 has a high peak at about 5.21 nm and 9.99 nm, the two samples all display micro-mesopore structures. However, both CoP-CQDs and CoP-NCQDs-1 contain two peaks at 3.30 nm and 61.10 nm, indicating that the sample has a small amount of macro-porous structure. In addition, the corresponding specific surface area and pore size as regards as four composites are summarized at Table S4. Therefore, the four composites all belong to the micro-mesoporous structures (2–50 nm), which is conducive to mass transfer. Different specific surface areas have an impact on the reactive active sites of the composites. CoP-NCQDs-3 has the largest specific surface area of 221.09 m2/g, while CoP-CQDs have the minimum of 165.85 m2/g.

To evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of CoP catalysts with different CQDs for OER, a convenient three-electrode system was used to test the electrochemical performance in O2-saturated KOH electrolyte (pH = 14). The corresponding test parameters and electrode preparation were described in the supporting information. All measured potentials of the LSV tests have been corrected with 90% iR compensation if not explicitly specified, where R is the solution resistance. The scanning rate of the LSV curves is 5 mV s−1 from 0.1 to 1 V (Fig. S8). Surprisingly, CoP-NCQDs-3 composite showed significantly improved OER activity. At the current density (j) of 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential was only 217.8 mV, while the overpotential (Fig. 5 a) of pure CoP, CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1 and CoP-NCQDs-2 was 489.8, 394.8, 314.8 and 268.8 mV, respectively. Its OER activity was better than that of all other materials, and this activity is significantly better than that of the existing technical materials listed in Table S5. The LSV results were further analyzed to obtain the Tafel diagram (Fig. S9), which shows that the CoP-NCQDs-3 composite has the lowest Tafel slope value (17.9 mV dec-1) compared to CoP-CQDs (73.7 mV dec-1), CoP-NCQDs-1 (67.3 mV dec-1), and CoP-NCQDs-2 (44.6 mV dec-1). These results suggest that the CoP-NCQDs-3 composite exhibits the fastest kinetics [51]. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were studied (Fig. S10) and the double layer capacitance (Cdl) was calculated from the CV curve of the non-Faraday region, which could reflect the electrochemical surface area. The Cdl value (Fig. 5b) shows that CoP-NCQDs-3 is 25.1 mF cm−2, and CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1 and CoP-NCQDs-2 are 6.0, 10.4 and 12.9 mF cm−2, respectively. This result shows that CoP-NCQDs-3 has the highest electrochemical surface area. For intuitive explanation, the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) and roughness factor (RF) are further calculated based on Cdl. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the CoP-NCQDs-3 composite exhibits an ECSA of 44.3 cm2 and an RF of 627.5, which are the highest among the tested samples. Additionally, it has the greatest roughness and largest specific surface area, which could lead to more exposed active sites and further enhance its electrocatalytic activity. These results are consistent with the BET test results. To understand the electron transfer resistance, the Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 5d. It shows that CoP-NCQDs-3 composite has the smallest diameter of the semicircle than CoP-CQDs, CoP-NCQDs-1 and CoP-NCQDs-2, indicating that the electron conduction of CoP-NCQDs-3 is the fastest, which is beneficial to improve the electrocatalytic performance. According to Table S1, it is obvious that pyrrole is the most beneficial to improve the electrocatalytic performance. In addition, as shown in Fig. S11, the long-term durability of CoP-NCQDs-3 for OER was evaluated through a chronoamperometry measurement for 20 h at 10 mA cm−2. The results show that CoP-NCQDs-3 only slightly decreases and can work almost stably with a certain degree of stability. In order to elucidate the reason for the decrease in current density after OER stability testing, TEM and XPS spectra were used to track the structural changes of CoP-NCQDs-3 before and after stability testing. The TEM and HRTEM spectra (Fig. S12) revealed that the catalyst remained aggregated in the form of a nanosheet, with NCQDs adhering to the surface of the CoP sheets. However, noteworthy changes in the morphology of the nanoflower and a significant reduction in the stacking density were observed. Furthermore, the valence states of surface elements of the CoP-NCQDs-3 catalyst were probed using Co 2p spectra both before and after OER stability testing. A slight negative shift in the peak of Co 2p (Fig. S13) was observed, along with a marked increase in the peak intensity of Co3+. This can be attributed to the oxidation of Co2+ to Co3+, which is converted into CoOOH [53,59,60]. Previous studies indicate that CoP undergoes a phase transition to CoOOH after OER stability testing on the catalyst. In order to further determine the metal active sites of CoP-NCQDs-3 for the OER. SCN ions poisoning experiment was carried out (Fig. S14). When SCN ions poisoning agent is added to the electrolyte, the OER current density for CoP-NCQDs-3 is significantly attenuated due to blockage of Co metal sites (Fig. S14, Insert), verified that Co metal is the true active sites. It has been proved above that NCQDs can improve the structure and effectively increase the exposure of active sites to improve the electron conductivity [15]. The results of SCN ions-poisoning experiment showed that the synergistic interaction between the Co atoms and NCQDs in CoP-NCQDs-3 to enhance the OER activity of the catalyst. And that further reveals the key role of Co metal sites and nitrogen doping in the electrocatalytic OER process [61].

To sum up, these results show that CoP-NCQDs-3 has excellent applicability to OER electrocatalysis under alkaline conditions. It has been proved that the π electrons in CQDs are conjugated with the solitary pair electrons in N atoms, making CQDs appear electron-rich [62], which leads to the excellent electrochemical and catalytic performance of CoP-NCQDs. Among the different N-doped forms, firstly, pyridine N is close to the edge of CQDs, which leads to structural defects [63,64], and is conducive to exposing the edge plane and amplifying the reactivity of the edge position [65]. Secondly, pyrrole N can be used as an electron donor, which can significantly adjust the electronic structure of the CoP center, making the Co atom in an electron-rich state. Finally, amine N and graphite N can reduce the bandgap, and CoP and CQDs form strong electronic interaction through Co-N-C covalent bond to enhance the conductivity. The electrochemical performance of the electrocatalyst appears to be intricately linked to the interplay between the nitrogen atom doping mode and the distinctive role of pyrrole nitrogen. Indeed, our findings suggest that pyrrole nitrogen may play a pivotal role in governing the observed synergistic effects.

In this work, we obtained NCQDs with different nitrogen doping types, namely graphite/amine N, pyridine N and pyrrole/amine/pyridine N. Then NCQDs are loaded into CoP through simple hydrothermal and phosphating treatment to form CoP-NCQDs catalysts. Based on the experimental findings, it can be surmised that the electrochemical performance of CoP-NCQDs is not solely contingent upon the N content in CQDs, but is also markedly influenced by the specific type of N doping. Both intranuclear and surface doping are conducive to structural defects or electronic conduction, and synergistically improve the electrochemical performance. Among them, CoP modified with pyrrole/amine/pyridine N doped CQDs (NCQDs-3) showed significantly improved electrocatalytic activity. In OER reaction, CoP-NCQDs-3 has the lowest over-potential, the largest specific surface area of electrochemical activity, the smallest charge transfer resistance and good stability. This work represents an initial exploration into the nitrogen doping types, prompting researchers to delve deeper into the design of electrocatalysts and encourage the development of high-performance, low-cost, and facile preparation methods for metal-free electrocatalysts.
Data will be made available on request.
Shuang Wu: Writing – review & editing. Xinyu Wang: . Yaqing Zhu: . Pinyi He: . Xu Yu: . Fu Qin: . Yongkang Yao: . Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22275035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110872.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Industrially promising IrNi-FeNi3 hybrid nanosheets for overall water splitting catalysis at large current density
Appl. Catal. B: Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, G. Qian, Q. Xu, H. Zhang, F. Shen, L. Luo, S. Yin, Industrially promising IrNi-FeNi3 hybrid nanosheets for overall water splitting catalysis at large current density, Appl. Catal. B: Environ. 286 (2021) 119881, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2021.119881.
